data.title: WeRide enables robotaxi booking via Tencent's WeChat in China
data.description:
• WeRide (WRD) is making robotaxi booking effortless in China via Tencent's (TCEHY) WeChat app.
  • Residents and visitors in WeRide’s robotaxi operating areas, including Guangzhou’s Huangpu district and Beijing’s Yizhuang district, can now book a ride directly through WeChat, without having to download a separate app.
  • By searching for the "WeRide Go" Mini Program on WeChat, users can instantly request a robotaxi.
  • WRD +1.59% premarket on Wednesday.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Chinese auto sales and exports head for slowdown, industry warns
data.description:
China’s vehicle sales and exports are expected to slow due to weak demand and ongoing external uncertainties, according to a major Chinese industry association.

Vehicle sales are expected to grow 1% this year, cooling from 9.4% last year, Reuters reported, citing data from the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers.

Sales growth of electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids is set to slow to 15.2% from 28.2%, while vehicle exports are likely to rise 4.3% after a stronger-than-expected 21.1% growth in 2025, CAAM data showed on Wednesday.

Further, it said the regulation over zero-mileage used cars, a tactic used by automakers to sell new cars as second-hand vehicles at heavily discounted prices, will intensify inventory pressure at home in the short term, the report added.

Related tickers: VanEck Low Carbon Energy ETF (SMOG), State Street SPDR S&P Kensho Smart Mobility ETF (HAIL), Great Wall Motor Company (GWLLF).

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures tread cautiously ahead of slew of economic reports
data.description:
Stock index futures were muted on Wednesday as investors awaited a slew of economic data, including the wholesale inflation report. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) slipped -0.03%, Dow futures (INDU) fell -0.08%, while Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) edged fractionally higher by +0.001%.

Treasury yields moved lower across the curve. The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell to 3.53%, down 0.34%. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) declined to 4.17%, losing 0.26%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) eased to 4.83%, down 0.21%.

The December producer price index will be released before the opening bell. PPI-FD is expected to be up the same 0.3% on the month and 2.7% on the year in November after 0.3% and 2.7% in October.

The retail sales and current account reports will land at the same time. Existing home sales and business inventories will come in during market hours. There are also a few Federal Reserve speakers lined up. 

Top gainers in premarket trading included Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) +3.26%, Edwards Lifesciences (EW) +2.70%, and Brown & Brown (BRO) +1.82%. Decliners included PACCAR (PCAR) -5.61%, Expeditors International of Washington (EXPD) -4.77%, and Agilent Technologies (A) -4.24%.

data.title: BP to book up to $5B in Q4 impairment charges tied to transition businesses
data.description:
BP (BP) said it expects Q4 results to include post-tax impairment charges of $4B to $5B, mainly tied to BP’s transition businesses.

“These charges are primarily attributable to the gas and low carbon energy segment and are excluded from underlying replacement cost profit,” the oil giant said.

Upstream production in Q4 is expected to be broadly flat versus the prior quarter. Oil production is seen as flat, while gas & low-carbon energy output is expected to be lower.

In the gas and low-carbon energy segment, price realizations are expected to impact results by $0.1–$0.3 billion compared with the prior quarter, reflecting changes in natural gas prices outside Henry Hub. Gas marketing and trading is expected to be average.

In customers and products segment, results are expected to reflect:

Customers: seasonally lower volumes and broadly flat fuel margins.

Products: stronger refining margins of about $0.1 billion, partly offset by higher turnaround activity and temporary capacity reductions following a fire at the Whiting refinery. Oil trading results are expected to be weak.

Net debt at the end of Q4 is expected to be in the range of $22B to 23B compared to $26.1B at the end of Q3.

The firm also expects a larger underlying effective tax rate for the full year 2025 of around 42%, compared to the previous guidance of ~40% primarily due to changes in the geographical mix of profits.

data.title: RWE sells 50% stake in Norfolk Vanguard offshore wind projects to KKR
data.description:
• RWE (RWEOY) (RWNEF) (RWNFF) said on Wednesday that it will sell a 50% stake in its Norfolk Vanguard West and East offshore wind projects to KKR (KKR).
  • The announcement follows RWE’s success in a major UK renewable energy auction.
  • RWE has been awarded 'Contracts for Difference' at a strike price of £91.20 per megawatt hour for its Norfolk Vanguard East and Norfolk Vanguard West, its two Dogger Bank South, and the Awel y Môr offshore wind development projects, with a combined capacity of 6.9 gigawatts.
  • RWE and KKR have agreed on a long-term partnership to jointly realize the Norfolk Vanguard East and Norfolk Vanguard West projects.
  • The transaction is expected to close in summer 2026.

data.title: Europe indexes in green after softer U.S. inflation
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.30% to 10,168. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.07% to 25,439.

France (CAC:IND) +0.49% to 8,388.

In other parts of Europe, total orders received by the Swedish industry jumped 23% Y/Y in November.

Household spending in Sweden increased 1.0% M/M in November.

Consumer prices in Finland rose 0.2% Y/Y in December.

The Netherlands’ trade surplus narrowed to €10.7B in November.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) traded 0.26% higher around €612, after touching fresh highs in the prior session, as the recent rally showed signs of losing momentum. Investors digested U.S. inflation data that came largely in line with expectations and did little to shift Fed outlook.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down less than 1 basis point to 4.17%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 2.81%.

UK's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 4.40%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Bill Pulte questions home builders' stock buybacks amid high housing costs - report
data.description:
The Trump administration could target home builders over their stock buybacks as housing costs remain high, following recent restrictions imposed on defense contractors' share repurchases, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Bill Pulte, director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, told WSJ that home builders have purposely kept prices high.

"They're making, in some cases, more money than they've ever made, and they're buying back stock like never before," he said. "It's something that we're studying, how much money they're spending on buybacks."

Pulte called on home builders to help lower prices and build more homes, hinting at penalties for companies that didn't help the administration's push to ease housing costs.

He said support for Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) shouldn't be "used to fund buybacks at the expense of Americans who need to get in homes."

"Read between the lines on that one," said Pulte. "Our preference is to use carrots, but sticks are on the table."

D.R. Horton (DHI) repurchased $4.3B in stock in its fiscal year, while Lennar (LEN) spent $1.7B. PulteGroup (PHM) spent $900M through the first nine months of 2025.

Last week, President Trump signed an executive order that restricted defense contractors from paying dividends or buying back stock till "they are able to produce a superior product, on time and on budget."

Trump is planning to unveil more housing and affordability plans in the coming weeks, after he said he is taking steps to ban large institutional investors from buying more single-family homes.

data.title: AI startup accused by Palantir claims lawsuit aims to scare employees from leaving
data.description:
• Three former Palantir Technologies (PLTR) employees say the company is trying to use the courts to squash their startup and deny poaching staff and intellectual property from it, Bloomberg News reported.
  • Palantir (PLTR), the data analytics company whose chairman is Peter Thiel, filed a revised lawsuit against the trio last month and asked a judge to bar Hirsh Jain from working at Percepta, which he co-founded, or for the AI startup’s venture capital backer, General Catalyst, for 12 months, the report added.
  • “In truth, Palantir is looking to scare others away from leaving and destroy Percepta before it can grow further,” lawyers for the startup said in the filing.
  • In October, Palantir filed a lawsuit against Percepta, claiming that its co-founders—Hirsh Jain and Radha Jain (unrelated)—misappropriated trade secrets and breached nonsolicitation agreements.
  • Palantir’s (PLTR) lawsuit is part of a growing wave of legal disputes in the AI sector over trade secrets and anti-competitive practices. Elon Musk’s xAI has sued former employees and rivals for alleged violations, while data-labeling startup Scale AI sued competitor Mercor and a former employee who joined Mercor for allegedly stealing trade secrets.

data.title: Coca-Cola axes Costa Coffee sale over lower bids - report
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) has cancelled its plan to sell Costa Coffee after private equity bids fell short of expectations, according to a Financial Times report that cites people familiar with the matter.

The soft drink maker ended negotiations in December. TDR Capital and Bain Capital’s (BCSF) special situations fund were among the bidders in the final round of negotiations, the report added.

Coca-Cola (KO) sought around £2B, roughly half the £3.9B it paid to acquire Costa from Whitbread in 2018.

The scrapped sale precedes a leadership shift, with COO Henrique Braun replacing James Quincey as CEO in March 2026; Quincey called Costa underperforming last year. The company may revisit divestiture plan in the medium term, a source added.

data.title: Caledonia Mining guides lower FY26 gold production from Blanket mine, flags higher costs
data.description:
Caledonia Mining (CMCL) expects to produce 72,000-76,500 ounces of gold from its Blanket Mine in Zimbabwe in FY 2026, with the quarterly production profile expected to be stronger in the second half of the year as higher-grade areas increasingly come on stream.

Total group capital expenditure is projected to be US$162.5M. Cost guidance for the year reflects higher direct operating costs at the mine compared with the first nine months of 2025, primarily due to inflation and increased costs in mining, milling and processing, and engineering departments.

Blanket gold production totaled 76,213 ounces in FY 2025, meeting the company’s increased guidance range of 75,500 to 79,500 ounces and broadly in line with output from the previous two years. Fourth-quarter 2025 production came in at 17,367 ounces, down from 19,841 ounces in Q4 2024.

Production in the second half of 2025 was adversely affected by lower tonnages from higher-grade areas (which is being addressed) and interruptions in the electricity supply at the end of the quarter.

Separately, Caledonia Mining (CMCL) said James Mufara, chief operating officer, has left the company. The company confirms that its operations continue to be managed by the existing executive and operating team, and the operations and strategy remain unchanged. It does not currently intend to appoint another COO.
